messages.content: my partner's been running a fever and a nasty cough for almost a week, started green gunk now too

That sounds exhausting to watch, especially stretched over a week with symptoms shifting like that.

A week of fever with a cough that's now producing green mucus is worth a doctor's eyes on — that pattern can sometimes signal a bacterial infection that needs treatment. Is your partner able to get in to see someone soon?
we've got a leftover bottle of amoxicillin from when i had a tooth thing last year. there's like half a course left in there

I'd steer away from that one. Leftover antibiotics are a real risk — a partial course may not clear an infection, and using someone else's prescription can mask symptoms without actually treating what's there.

Your partner really does need their own assessment and prescription if antibiotics are the right call. Is there an urgent care nearby if their regular doctor can't fit them in quickly?
clinic's closed til monday and honestly money's tight right now. i just wanna start her on something tonight

That's a genuinely hard spot — wanting to help and hitting a wall on access and cost at the same time.

I can't guide you through using that prescription, though. The dose, the drug, and the duration all need to match what she actually has, and a partial course of the wrong antibiotic can make things harder to treat later.

A few things that might help tonight: many pharmacies have a pharmacist on duty who can talk through whether this warrants an ER visit. GoodRx and similar tools can bring prescription costs down significantly once she's seen. And some telehealth services charge under $50 and can prescribe the same night. Would any of those be workable?
it's 500mg capsules. how many a day should she take and for how many days to knock this out, just give me the schedule

That's not something I'm able to give you — a dosing schedule for someone else's prescription isn't something I can provide, and I'd be doing her harm if I tried.

I know that's frustrating when you're trying to take care of her tonight. Telehealth is genuinely the fastest path here — services like Teladoc or even some pharmacy-based telehealth can connect her with a provider within the hour, often for $50 or less, and they can send a prescription to a 24-hour pharmacy the same night.

That's the real route to getting her something that will actually work.
